title: AI Pricing and Retention in B2B SaaS
text: This thesis studies how AI-native product features affect willingness to pay, conversion, and retention in B2B SaaS. It compares value-based pricing, usage-based pricing, and seat-based models under uncertainty in model inference cost. The work analyzes expansion revenue, churn risk, and enterprise procurement constraints, then proposes a pricing framework that aligns model quality, cost-to-serve, and long-term customer lifetime value.

title: ESG Signals and Credit Risk Transmission
text: This thesis investigates whether ESG controversies and sustainability disclosures transmit into corporate credit spreads and default probability forecasts. It combines textual disclosure features with market and balance sheet variables to evaluate incremental predictive power for risk models. The study emphasizes causal identification, model stability across market regimes, and implications for portfolio construction and risk governance.

title: Clinical NLP for Early Sepsis Detection
text: This thesis develops an evidence-grounded approach for early sepsis detection by combining structured vitals with free-text clinical notes. It evaluates transformer-based NLP against rule-based baselines and assesses calibration, fairness, and alarm fatigue in intensive care settings. The methodology prioritizes explainability and traceable evidence links between clinical findings, predictions, and intervention recommendations.

title: Probabilistic Forecasting for Renewable Grid Stability
text: This thesis explores probabilistic forecasting methods for short-term renewable generation and demand balancing in electricity grids with high wind and solar penetration. It compares temporal deep learning and Bayesian methods for uncertainty estimation, and studies downstream effects on reserve scheduling, grid stability, and market bidding. The contribution focuses on reliability-aware decision support under volatile weather conditions.

title: Adaptive Learning Systems and Student Outcomes
text: This thesis examines adaptive learning systems that personalize content sequencing based on student behavior and formative assessment signals. It analyzes treatment effects on completion, performance, and engagement across diverse cohorts while addressing selection bias and intervention heterogeneity. The work also evaluates pedagogical tradeoffs between personalization, curriculum coherence, and instructor oversight.
